7	# Feature: DDx Agent Service (consumer of ddx-agent contract)
8	
9	**ID:** FEAT-006
10	**Status:** In Progress (migrating to ddx-agent CONTRACT-003)
11	**Priority:** P0
12	**Owner:** DDx Team
13	
14	## Overview
15	
16	DDx invokes LLMs through the `ddx-agent` module, defined by
17	**CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service** in the `~/Projects/agent` repo
18	(`docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md`).
19	
20	That contract is the entire boundary. DDx's CLI commands (`ddx agent run`,
21	`ddx agent execute-bead`, `ddx work`, `ddx agent providers`, `ddx agent models`,
22	`ddx agent route-status`, `ddx agent check`) are thin wrappers that call into
23	the `agentlib.DdxAgent` interface and render its results.
24	
25	## DDx-side responsibilities
26	
27	DDx owns the bead-driven workflow surface. It does NOT own:
28	- Harness orchestration (claude, codex, opencode, pi, gemini, native)
29	- Provider routing
30	- Model catalog
31	- Compaction
32	- Tool registration
33	- Session-log shape
34	
35	Those all live inside ddx-agent per CONTRACT-003.
36	
37	DDx owns:
38	
39	- **Bead-driven invocation.** `ddx agent execute-bead`, `ddx work`, and the
40	  server's queue-drain worker translate bead state into `ExecuteRequest`
41	  values and surface results back into the bead tracker.
42	- **Execute-bead orchestration.** Worktree creation, base-revision pinning,
43	  result landing (merge / preserve / no-changes), gate evaluation, evidence
44	  bundle capture. The agent provides the LLM execution; DDx provides the
45	  git-aware orchestration.
46	- **Evidence and session capture.** DDx writes `.ddx/executions/<id>/`
47	  bundles with prompts, manifests, and result artifacts. The agent's session
48	  log path (returned in `ExecuteResponse.SessionLogPath`) is captured into
49	  the bundle.
50	- **Profile policy at the request level.** DDx selects `Profile`, `Effort`,
51	  `Permissions` per bead (or per CLI invocation) based on bead metadata
52	  and user input. The agent receives those as `ExecuteRequest` fields and
53	  performs the routing.
54	
55	## Profile Routing
56	
57	`ddx work --profile default` is the primary queue-drain invocation. DDx maps
58	the user-facing profile to an ordered tier ladder and resolves each tier through
59	the agent service with the configured provider affinity and capability gating.
60	
61	| Profile | Ladder | Intent |
62	| --- | --- | --- |
63	| `default` | `[cheap, standard, smart]` | Local first, then subscription cloud, then high-quality cloud when earlier tiers fail. This is the common throughput-per-dollar path. |
64	| `cheap` | `[cheap]` | Local only. Never escalates; if local cannot serve the bead, the bead is unclaimed and waits. |
65	| `fast` | `[fast, smart]` | Cloud-fast first, skipping local warmup; escalates to smart on failure. |
66	| `smart` | `[smart]` | High-quality cloud from the start. No escalation. |
67	
68	The `.ddx/config.yaml` field `agent.routing.profile_ladders` can override the
69	ordered tier list per profile. The legacy flat `agent.routing.profile_priority`
70	is still read as the `default` profile fallback and emits a deprecation warning;
71	new configs should use `profile_ladders.default`. `agent.routing.model_overrides`
72	can map a ladder tier such as `cheap`, `standard`, `fast`, or `smart` to a
73	concrete model reference before DDx asks the agent service to resolve the route.
74	
75	Escalation advances to the next tier for `execution_failed`,
76	`land_conflict`, `post_run_check_failed`, and
77	`structural_validation_failed`. `no_changes` keeps the existing cooldown and
78	satisfaction-adjudication path rather than consuming a higher tier.
79	
80	### Conflict-recovery structured outcomes (bead ddx-0097af14)
81	
82	Before parking a bead on `land_conflict` (or on `execution_failed` when the
83	agent produced a commit before failing), the execute-loop now attempts to reuse
84	the preserved iteration ref rather than discarding the agent's work:
85	
86	1. **3-way ort auto-merge** (`git merge --no-ff -s ort -X ours`). If clean,
87	   the bead closes as `success`.
88	2. **Focused conflict-resolve agent** (if `ConflictResolver` is configured on
89	   the worker). If clean, the bead closes as `success`.
90	3. **Park with structured outcome.** Two new statuses replace the generic
91	   reopen when both recovery paths fail:
92	
93	| Status | Event kind | Meaning |
94	| --- | --- | --- |
95	| `land_conflict_unresolvable` | `land-conflict-unresolvable` | All auto-recovery failed; retryable after 15-min cooldown. |
96	| `land_conflict_needs_human` | `land-conflict-needs-human` | Focused-resolve agent returned BLOCKING; human input required. |
97	
98	Both statuses use `LandConflictCooldown` (15 min) rather than the 24h cap used
99	for `push_failed`, because land conflicts typically unblock quickly as sibling
100	beads advance the base branch.
101	
102	## Asking ddx-agent for changes
103	
104	When DDx needs new behavior from the agent ŌĆö a new method, a new field on
105	`ExecuteRequest`, a new event type, a new policy knob ŌĆö file a PR against
106	CONTRACT-003 in the agent repo. Maintainers decide whether the surface grows.
107	
108	Do not import agent internal packages. They live under `internal/` and the
109	Go compiler blocks external imports after agent v0.5.0 ships.
110	
111	## Migration status
112	
113	DDx is currently migrating from the legacy DDx-internal routing/harness
114	implementation to the CONTRACT-003 surface. Migration epic: `ddx-ac5c7fdb`.
115	Pre-migration, FEAT-006 documented harness orchestration responsibilities
116	that have since moved upstream.
117	
118	## Design References
119	
120	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-006-agent-session-capture.md` ŌĆö session log shape and evidence bundles
121	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-013-multi-agent-coordination.md` ŌĆö worktree-aware dispatch, concurrent bead safety
122	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-014-token-awareness.md` ŌĆö provider availability, quota, and signal model
123	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md` ŌĆö bead-level cost and rework derivation
124	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-020-multi-machine-coordinator-topology.md` ŌĆö host+user coordinator scoping
125	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-021-service-backed-multi-node-topology.md` ŌĆö service-backed node topology
126	- `docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-006-agent-session-capture.md` ŌĆö session log and artifact format
127	- `docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-010-executions.md` ŌĆö execution attempt bundles at `.ddx/executions/`
128	- `docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-18-ddx-agent-service-interface.md` ŌĆö current thin-consumer migration plan
129	- `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-006-agent-session-capture.md` ŌĆö session capture test coverage
130	- `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-014-token-awareness.md` ŌĆö token-awareness coverage
131	- `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-020-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md` ŌĆö routing and catalog resolution coverage
132

9	# Feature: Executions (Definitions and Runs)
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-010
12	**Status:** In Progress
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	DDx executions provide a workflow-agnostic way to run repository-local
19	operations, capture their raw logs, normalize their structured outputs, and
20	retain immutable run history over time.
21	
22	Artifacts define what matters and how it relates to the rest of the document
23	graph. Execution definitions define how an artifact or group of artifacts is
24	evaluated. Execution runs record what happened when that definition was
25	invoked.
26	
27	This gives DDx a reusable runtime substrate for metrics, acceptance checks,
28	test-case evaluation, and other evidence-producing operations without teaching
29	DDx about workflow phases or tool-specific actions.
30	
31	## Problem Statement
32	
33	**Current situation:** DDx already knows artifacts and their relationships, and
34	it already has specialized runtime surfaces such as `ddx agent`. But there is
35	no generic execution primitive for non-agent operations or for linking runtime
36	evidence back to artifacts in a uniform way.
37	
38	**Pain points:**
39	- Artifact documents get overloaded with runtime details that do not belong in
40	  the authority graph
41	- Metrics, acceptance checks, and similar operations fall back to ad hoc files,
42	  one-off scripts, or tool-specific logs
43	- There is no shared way to capture raw logs and structured result data for one
44	  execution
45	- There is no durable, queryable run history linked to artifact IDs
46	- Agent-backed and command-backed evidence do not share a common execution
47	  record model
48	
49	**Desired outcome:** A generic `ddx exec` surface that stores machine-readable
50	execution definitions, runs them reproducibly, captures logs and structured
51	results, and persists immutable history linked to artifacts by ID.
52	
53	## Execution Model
54	
55	DDx separates three concerns:
56	
57	1. **Artifact** ŌĆö a declarative node in the document graph (`MET-*`, `FEAT-*`,
58	   `ADR-*`, or any other project convention)
59	2. **Execution definition** ŌĆö a machine-readable runtime contract linked to one
60	   or more artifact IDs
61	3. **Execution run** ŌĆö an immutable record of one invocation of one execution
62	   definition
63	
64	### Projections and Convenience Surfaces
65	
66	DDx may expose domain-specific projections over execution definitions and runs
67	when a project benefits from a narrower vocabulary, but those projections do
68	not own separate storage or a separate runtime substrate.
69	
70	Examples:
71	- metrics project numeric observations, comparison results, and trends from
72	  execution runs linked to `MET-*` artifacts
73	- acceptance or test-case projections may present pass/fail evidence for
74	  artifacts such as `AC-*` or `TC-*`
75	- convenience commands such as `ddx metric` are optional wrappers over
76	  `ddx exec`; they must not introduce independent `.ddx/<domain>/` stores or
77	  bypass the generic execution record model
78	
79	### Execution Definition
80	
81	An execution definition describes:
82	- definition ID
83	- linked artifact ID or IDs
84	- executor kind (`command`, `agent`, future extension points)
85	- inputs such as command, prompt reference, working directory, environment, and
86	  timeout
87	- log capture policy
88	- structured result schema or parser contract
89	- optional evaluation rules such as pass/fail interpretation or scalar-field
90	  extraction
91	
92	Execution definitions may be authored as git-tracked documents (graph-authored
93	definitions, discovered via FEAT-007) or managed as DDx runtime records
94	(runtime-managed definitions stored in the `exec-definitions` collection).
95	Graph-authored definitions participate in ordinary DDx document indexing and
96	are the preferred source when using `ddx agent execute-bead`. Runtime-managed
97	definitions in the `exec-definitions` collection remain valid for `ddx exec`
98	operations that do not require graph discovery. In either case, execution
99	runs are always immutable runtime records in the exec-runs substrate.
100	
101	### Execution Run
102	
103	An execution run records:
104	- run ID
105	- definition ID
106	- linked artifact ID or IDs
107	- executor kind
108	- started/finished timestamps
109	- terminal status
110	- raw logs
111	- structured result payload
112	- attachment references for large captured bodies when needed
113	- provenance such as actor, host, git revision, and DDx version
114	
115	Runs are append-only evidence. A new run never rewrites a previous one.
116	The authoritative run metadata may be stored as a bead-backed record in a
117	dedicated execution collection, with named attachment files for large bodies,
118	as long as DDx preserves one coherent run identity and inspection model.
119	
120	This generic execution-run model is not the same as the tracked
121	`execute-bead` attempt bundle under `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`.
122	
123	- `exec-runs` stores reusable execution-run history for `ddx exec`,
124	  metrics, checks, and other generic execution surfaces
125	- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` stores one tracked execute-bead attempt
126	  bundle with prompt, manifest, result, checks, and provenance pointers
127	
128	`execute-bead` may consume graph-authored execution definitions and may emit
129	references to generic execution runs, but its tracked attempt evidence is a
130	separate artifact class because it is intended to be committed with landed or
131	preserved implementation work.
132	
133	## Requirements
134	
135	### Functional
136	
137	1. **Execution definition storage** ŌĆö DDx stores machine-readable execution definitions with stable IDs in repo-local storage
138	2. **Artifact linkage** ŌĆö each definition links explicitly to one or more artifact IDs from the DDx document graph
139	3. **Executor kinds** ŌĆö DDx supports built-in `command` and `agent` executor kinds, with room for future extension
140	4. **Definition validation** ŌĆö `ddx exec validate` checks schema, linked artifact IDs, executor configuration, and result contract
141	5. **Execution invocation** ŌĆö `ddx exec run <id>` executes one definition and creates one immutable run record
142	6. **Raw log capture** ŌĆö each run captures stdout/stderr or equivalent raw execution logs
143	7. **Structured result capture** ŌĆö each run stores machine-readable result data separate from raw logs
144	8. **Normalized terminal status** ŌĆö each run records whether it succeeded, failed, timed out, or errored before producing a result
145	9. **Append-only history** ŌĆö DDx retains ordered execution history without mutating prior runs
146	10. **History inspection** ŌĆö users and tools can query runs by artifact ID, definition ID, status, or recency
147	11. **Run detail inspection** ŌĆö users and tools can inspect one run's logs, structured result, and provenance
148	12. **Agent executor integration** ŌĆö agent-backed definitions can delegate to `ddx agent` and retain the underlying session identity and logs
149	13. **Configuration** ŌĆö storage roots, retention settings, and executor defaults are configurable in `.ddx/config.yaml`
150	14. **Projection support** ŌĆö DDx supports domain-specific read models over shared execution definitions and runs without introducing separate runtime stores
151	15. **Metric specialization** ŌĆö metrics may define numeric-result conventions, comparison behavior, and trend summaries as projections over execution runs linked to `MET-*` artifacts
152	16. **Compatibility and migration** ŌĆö when DDx replaces a specialized runtime surface with `ddx exec`, the feature must define either a migration path or an explicit backward-compatible read/write policy
153	17. **Attachment-backed evidence** ŌĆö DDx may store large run bodies such as logs or structured payloads in immutable attachment files referenced by run metadata rather than forcing all evidence inline
154	18. **Collection-backed storage** ŌĆö DDx may store execution definitions and runs as bead-schema records in dedicated runtime collections rather than inventing a separate metadata schema for each execution family
155	
156	### Non-Functional
157	
158	- **Determinism:** DDx must persist the exact logs and structured result associated with one invocation
159	- **Durability:** definition and run writes must be atomic or serialized so concurrent writers cannot leave partial records behind
160	- **Observability:** stored runs must be human-readable enough for debugging and machine-parseable enough for automation
161	- **Portability:** definitions and runs remain repo-local and file-backed; no hosted service or database is required for v1
162	- **Low overhead:** execution bookkeeping adds minimal overhead beyond the underlying command or agent invocation
163	- **Payload resilience:** large prompt, response, and log bodies must not require rewriting a shared history file to persist one run safely
164	
165	## CLI Commands
166	
167	```bash
168	ddx exec list [--artifact ID]                          # list execution definitions
169	ddx exec show <definition-id>                          # show one definition
170	ddx exec validate <definition-id>                      # validate definition and links
171	ddx exec run <definition-id>                           # execute and persist a run
172	ddx exec log <run-id>                                  # show raw logs for one run
173	ddx exec result <run-id> [--json]                      # show structured result
174	ddx exec history [--artifact ID] [--definition ID]     # inspect historical runs
175	```
176	
177	## Server Endpoints (FEAT-002 integration)
178	
179	| MCP Tool | HTTP Endpoint | Description |
180	|----------|--------------|-------------|
181	| `ddx_exec_definitions` | `GET /api/exec/definitions` | List execution definitions |
182	| `ddx_exec_show` | `GET /api/exec/definitions/:id` | Show one execution definition |
183	| `ddx_exec_history` | `GET /api/exec/runs` | List historical execution runs |
184	| `ddx_exec_run` | `GET /api/exec/runs/:id` | Show one run's structured result and metadata |
185	
186	The HTTP/MCP surface is read-only for v1. Execution invocation remains CLI-only.
187	
188	## User Stories
189	
190	### US-090: Developer Runs a Metric-Backed Execution
191	**As a** developer tracking a metric artifact
192	**I want** to execute its linked DDx definition and store the observed result
193	**So that** I can review the logs and structured numeric output later
194	
195	**Acceptance Criteria:**
196	- Given an execution definition linked to `MET-001`, when I run `ddx exec run <definition-id>`, then DDx records a new immutable run with raw logs and structured result data
197	- Given the run completes, when I inspect its history, then I can find it by `MET-001` and by the definition ID
198	
199	### US-091: Developer Reviews Acceptance Evidence Over Time
200	**As a** developer evaluating an acceptance artifact
201	**I want** to inspect historical pass/fail runs and their details
202	**So that** I can see whether the criterion has been stable or regressed
203	
204	**Acceptance Criteria:**
205	- Given an execution definition linked to an acceptance artifact, when repeated runs occur, then DDx preserves ordered history rather than overwriting the last result
206	- Given I inspect one run, then I can see its status, logs, structured result, and provenance
207	
208	### US-092: Workflow Tool Queries Reusable Execution Evidence
209	**As** a workflow tool or check runner
210	**I want** to query execution history by artifact ID
211	**So that** I can build higher-level decisions without inventing my own log format
212	
213	**Acceptance Criteria:**
214	- Given execution history exists for an artifact, when a tool queries DDx, then it receives structured run metadata and result payloads
215	- Given the execution was agent-backed, then the returned run data retains a link to the underlying agent session
216	
217	### US-093: Developer Uses a Metric Convenience Surface Without Forking Storage
218	**As a** developer working with metrics frequently
219	**I want** any optional metric-focused command surface to resolve through `ddx exec`
220	**So that** metrics stay aligned with the generic execution model
221	
222	**Acceptance Criteria:**
223	- Given a metric-linked execution definition exists, when I use an optional metric convenience command, then it resolves through the same execution definitions and run history used by `ddx exec`
224	- Given metric history exists, then there is no separate authoritative `.ddx/metrics/` runtime store that can drift from `.ddx/exec/`
225	
226	### US-094: Operator Migrates From a Specialized Runtime Store
227	**As a** repo operator adopting the execution model
228	**I want** a defined migration or compatibility policy for older specialized runtime data
229	**So that** adopting `ddx exec` does not strand existing metric evidence
230	
231	**Acceptance Criteria:**
232	- Given a repository contains prior metric runtime data, when DDx adopts `ddx exec`, then new execution and metric writes land in bead-backed collections and legacy `.ddx/exec/` or `.ddx/metrics/` data remains readable as a fallback during migration
233	- Given the chosen policy, then the behavior is documented and testable rather than implicit
234	
235	### US-095: Execute-bead Compatibility and Definition Source Priority
236	**As** a workflow tool invoking execute-bead
237	**I want** DDx to resolve graph-authored execution definitions as authoritative
238	**So that** runtime behavior matches the documents in git
239	
240	**Acceptance Criteria:**
241	- Given a graph-authored execution definition exists for an artifact, when `ddx exec validate` or `ddx exec run` resolves that artifact's definition, then the graph-authored document takes precedence over any runtime-managed definition in the `exec-definitions` collection.
242	- Given only a runtime-managed definition exists for an artifact, when `ddx exec run` is invoked, then it proceeds using the runtime-managed definition without error.
243	- Given an execution definition is marked `required: true`, when its execution run terminates with a non-success status, then the result is classified as merge-blocking and any execute-bead workflow consuming it preserves rather than lands.
244	- Given a metric-producing execution run completes, when `ddx metric` queries for that metric, then the result is served from the `exec-runs` collection ŌĆö no `.ddx/metrics/` directory is created or required.
245	- Given an agent-backed execution run completes, when the run record is inspected, then it retains a stable link to the underlying agent session ID resolvable via `ddx agent log`.
246	- Given `ddx agent execute-bead` triggers execution documents, when the resulting runs are queried, then they appear in `ddx exec history` and `ddx metric` output through the standard inspection surfaces.
247	
248	## Dependencies
249	
250	- FEAT-005 (Artifacts) ŌĆö execution definitions link runtime behavior to artifact IDs
251	- FEAT-006 (Agent Service) ŌĆö provides the `agent` executor kind and canonical agent session logs
252	- DDx CLI infrastructure (config loading, command factory)
253	
254	## Out of Scope
255	
256	- Workflow-specific action semantics such as phase routing or tool-specific issue closing rules ŌĆö delegated to workflow tools
257	- **Autonomy semantics and escalation policy** ŌĆö DDx does not define what autonomy levels mean or when to escalate; those are delegated to workflow tools
258	- **When to invoke execution and what to do with results** ŌĆö DDx provides the execution substrate; workflow tools decide when to run executions and how to act on outcomes
259	- Automatic generation of execution definitions from artifact prose
260	- Server-side execution invocation over HTTP/MCP
261	- Centralized hosted execution history storage
262	- Visual dashboards or methodology-specific scoring rules
263	- Separate domain-specific runtime stores that duplicate the authority of `ddx exec`
264

114		contextBudget, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("context-budget")

126			Effort:        effort,
127			ContextBudget: contextBudget,

131		}

133		if err != nil {

139			WorkerID:   os.Getenv("DDX_WORKER_ID"),
140			BeadEvents: bead.NewStore(filepath.Join(projectRoot, ".ddx")),
141		}

143		var gitOps agent.GitOps = &agent.RealGitOps{}
144		if f.executeBeadGitOverride != nil {
145			gitOps = f.executeBeadGitOverride
146		}
147	
148		var orchestratorGitOps agent.OrchestratorGitOps = &agent.RealOrchestratorGitOps{}
149		if f.executeBeadOrchestratorGitOverride != nil {
150			orchestratorGitOps = f.executeBeadOrchestratorGitOverride
151		}
152	
153		// Test injection seam: AgentRunnerOverride feeds canned *Result values
154		// from fakes without spinning up a real provider. Production callers
155		// leave this nil so ExecuteBead constructs an agent service from
156		// projectRoot internally.
157		if f.AgentRunnerOverride != nil {
158			runtime.AgentRunner = f.AgentRunnerOverride

162		// the execute-loop preflight so `ddx agent execute-bead --model <unroutable>`

168				return err
169			}
170		}

172		// Recover any orphaned worktrees from previous crashed runs before
173		// spawning a new worker. This is the parent's responsibility.
174		agent.RecoverOrphans(gitOps, projectRoot, beadID)

177		res, err := agent.ExecuteBeadWithConfig(cmd.Context(), projectRoot, beadID, rcfg, runtime, gitOps)

183		// preserve). Always run when we have a result ŌĆö the orchestrator handles
184		// all cases including no-changes and error (agent failed with no commits).
185		//

229			}
230		}

232		if err != nil {
233			_ = writeExecuteBeadResult(cmd, res, asJSON)
234			return err

13	# API/Interface Contract: Execute-Bead Supervisor
14	
15	## Purpose
16	
17	This contract defines the first shipped supervision loop for
18	`ddx agent execute-bead` style work. It is intentionally single-project scoped
19	and consumes DDx's generic execution readiness and execution-result surfaces
20	rather than any HELIX-specific hidden policy.
21	
22	The supervisor contract covers:
23	
24	- one project context at a time
25	- one ordinary executable bead at a time
26	- bead claim and selection flow
27	- structural execution validation
28	- delegated execute-bead execution
29	- observation of execute-bead result states
30	- operator controls and loop observability
31	
32	## Boundary
33	
34	DDx owns the generic substrate:
35	
36	- bead storage and claim state
37	- execution-definition validation and run recording
38	- git worktree mechanics
39	- hidden-ref preservation of non-landed iterations
40	
41	HELIX owns the workflow policy:
42	
43	- which beads are eligible for the loop
44	- when the loop should run
45	- retry policy after a failure
46	- what operator-facing automation wraps the loop
47	
48	The first shipped supervisor is intentionally **not** the epic worker. Epic
49	execution uses a separate worker mode because it needs a persistent branch,
50	worktree, and merge-commit landing contract.
51	
52	`ddx agent execute-bead` remains the single owner of required execution
53	document resolution, required post-run checks, merge-eligibility evaluation,
54	and land/preserve mechanics, including creation and cleanup of its isolated
55	worktree. The supervisor only orchestrates queue selection, invokes that
56	command from the project root, and records the result states emitted by that
57	command.
58	
59	The command requires a reproducible git base revision, not a pristine root
60	checkout. Tracked root changes may be checkpointed into an immutable base
61	commit before launch; ignored runtime scratch must not block the attempt.
62	
63	The first release does not require multi-project discovery, cross-project
64	scheduling, or multi-host coordination. A worker instance binds to exactly one
65	project context for the duration of a loop.
66	
67	Each attempt also has a tracked execution-evidence bundle under
68	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`. The supervisor may read and surface those
69	artifacts, but it does not reinterpret them as generic `exec-runs`
70	attachments; the bundle is the canonical tracked evidence for one
71	`execute-bead` attempt.
72	
73	The bundle contents and write order are owned by `execute-bead` and
74	specified in FEAT-006 ┬¦"Execute-Bead Evidence Bundle". At minimum, the
75	bundle contains `prompt.md`, `manifest.json`, and `result.json`, and is
76	committed alongside the iteration (landed or preserved). The supervisor
77	must not treat the absence of any of those files as a normal post-run
78	state.
79	
80	The prompt delivered to the agent for each attempt is compiled by the
81	**execute-bead prompt rationalizer** from bead fields and resolved governing
82	references (see FEAT-006 ┬¦"Prompt Rationalizer Contract"). The rationalizer
83	writes `prompt.md` to the bundle before the agent runs; this file is the
84	authoritative record of exactly what the agent received. The supervisor does
85	not author or modify the prompt.
86	
87	Each iteration commit (landed or preserved under a hidden ref) carries the
88	canonical Git trailer set defined in FEAT-006 ┬¦"Canonical Git trailers":
89	`Ddx-Attempt-Id`, `Ddx-Worker-Id`, `Ddx-Harness`, `Ddx-Model`, and
90	`Ddx-Result-Status`. The supervisor relies on `Ddx-Attempt-Id` and
91	`Ddx-Result-Status` when projecting its observability surface from commit
92	history, and treats the trailer set as authoritative alongside the
93	supervisor-visible `status` field emitted by `execute-bead`.
94	
95	## Single-Project State Machine
96	
97	The supervision loop is a bounded state machine that repeats until the queue is
98	drained, the operator stops it, or a fatal project error occurs.
99	
100	1. Resolve one project context from the current repository, explicit selector,
101	   or `ddx server` project binding.
102	2. Resolve the effective base revision and the governing execution contract
103	   snapshot that will govern this iteration.
104	3. Read the ready bead set for that project and order candidates by the
105	   supervisor's queue policy for that project context.
106	   - Ready non-epic beads are ordered ahead of ready epics at the same
107	     priority.
108	   - Open epics are excluded from this worker's launch set by default.
109	4. Run the generic execution-ready validator against the ordered candidate set
110	   to filter structural ineligible beads against the resolved base snapshot.
111	5. Claim the first validated bead atomically.
112	6. Run `ddx agent execute-bead` against the bead from the project root and
113	   capture its documented result schema.
114	7. Classify the outcome reported by `execute-bead` from the documented
115	   supervisor-visible `status` field:
116	   - structural validation failure before launch
117	   - execution failure
118	   - post-run check failure
119	   - land conflict after a successful attempt
120	   - success
121	8. Continue scanning the same project queue.
122	
123	The loop must never infer readiness from HELIX-specific hidden policy. It uses
124	the explicit queue ordering policy, the shared validator output, and the
125	documented result schema as its source of truth.
126	
127	## Epic Boundary
128	
129	Epics are first-class tracker items, but they are not consumed by the ordinary
130	single-ticket supervisor.
131	
132	- The single-ticket supervisor drains executable child beads and other
133	  non-epic work.
134	- A separate epic-scoped worker owns one persistent epic branch and worktree.
135	- That worker executes child beads sequentially inside the epic worktree,
136	  commits each child result on the epic branch, and merges the completed epic
137	  to the target branch with a regular merge commit.
138	
139	The epic worker's branch, worktree, child-execution, merge-gate, and final
140	merge-commit contract is defined in API-002 (Epic Worker Surfaces) and the
141	"Epic Execution Workflow" section of FEAT-006. The single-ticket supervisor
142	must observe these boundary rules even though it does not operate on epics
143	itself:
144	
145	1. **No epic claims.** This supervisor must never claim an epic bead. Ready
146	   epics are filtered out of its candidate set even when they pass the
147	   structural execution validator.
148	2. **Child beads during an active epic.** When a child bead belongs to an
149	   epic that is currently being executed by an epic worker, this supervisor
150	   must not claim that child. The epic worker is the authoritative consumer
151	   of in-flight epic children. The supervisor treats such children as
152	   already claimed by the epic worker.
153	3. **Child beads for idle epics.** Children of epics that have no active
154	   epic worker remain ineligible for this supervisor by default. Promoting
155	   an orphaned child to the single-ticket queue is a workflow-tool decision,
156	   not a supervisor decision.
157	4. **Child close semantics.** When the epic worker closes a child mid-epic,
158	   the child's result commit lives on the epic branch (`ddx/epics/<epic-id>`)
159	   and has not yet reached the target branch. This supervisor must treat
160	   such a child as "closed on epic" rather than "landed on target" when
161	   computing readiness for its own queue ŌĆö downstream beads that depend on a
162	   child closed on epic are not yet eligible for single-ticket execution on
163	   the target branch until the epic itself merges.
164	5. **Final landing is not this supervisor's job.** Landing the completed
165	   epic with a `--no-ff` merge commit is owned by the epic worker. This
166	   supervisor does not evaluate epic merge gates, does not rebase the epic
167	   branch, and does not perform the final merge.
168	
169	This contract therefore remains the single-ticket worker contract even after
170	epic execution is introduced elsewhere in the system.
171	
172	## Execute-Bead Result Schema
173	
174	The supervisor consumes only the documented result envelope emitted by
175	`ddx agent execute-bead`:
176	
177	- `status`: one of `structural_validation_failed`, `execution_failed`,
178	  `post_run_check_failed`, `land_conflict`, or `success`
179	- `detail`: optional operator-facing text for logging and diagnostics
180	
181	The supervisor must not infer state from free-form reason strings. It uses the
182	`status` field for control flow and may surface `detail` for observability.
183	
184	`execute-bead` result classification is based on the managed worktree outcome,
185	not solely on agent-authored commits. If the agent leaves tracked file edits
186	without creating commits, `execute-bead` synthesizes the result commit and
187	then evaluates land/preserve semantics. Only a truly clean managed worktree may
188	be reported as `no_changes`.
189	
190	## Validation And Retry Semantics
191	
192	Structural validation happens before any irreversible execution step.
193	
194	- If a bead fails structural validation, the supervisor records the blocker,
195	  unclaims the bead, and leaves it open for later correction.
196	- If execution starts and later fails, `execute-bead` preserves the iteration
197	  under a hidden ref using the documented naming scheme, and the supervisor
198	  records that result `status`.
199	- If post-run required checks fail, `execute-bead` preserves the iteration
200	  under a hidden ref, sets the documented failure `status`, and the supervisor
201	  records that result `status`.
202	- If a rebase or fast-forward land fails after a successful run, `execute-bead`
203	  preserves the iteration and the preserved iteration remains the canonical
204	  evidence for that attempt.
205	
206	Retry surface:
207	
208	- operators or workflow tooling fix the governing docs, bead content, or
209	  environment
210	- the bead is unclaimed and made ready again
211	- the next loop pass may claim it and create a new iteration
212	
213	Previous preserved refs remain immutable. A retry does not rewrite or reuse the
214	prior attempt.
215	
216	## Land And Preserve Semantics
217	
218	Success is only complete after the result is landed by rebase plus
219	fast-forward.
220	
221	- fetch origin before the rebase step so the local target tip reflects the
222	  latest remote state
223	- rebase the execution branch onto the latest target tip
224	- push `--ff-only` to origin; the remote's atomic ref-update is the
225	  serialization point for concurrent coordinators on other machines
226	- fast-forward the local target branch to match after a successful push
227	- reset the worker worktree to the updated branch tip after a successful land
228	- preserve the iteration under a hidden ref when the result cannot be landed or
229	  `--no-merge` semantics apply
230	
231	On push rejection (another coordinator landed first), the losing coordinator
232	preserves the iteration under `refs/ddx/iterations/` and unclaims the bead
233	without force-pushing. The multi-machine coordinator topology and the full
234	conflict and divergence recovery contract are specified in SD-020.
235	
236	These mechanics are owned by `ddx agent execute-bead`; the supervisor observes
237	the resulting landed or preserved state rather than performing them itself.
238	
239	The preserved ref is the durable evidence for any non-landed attempt. It keeps
240	the exact iteration commit, associated metadata, and inspection trail intact.
241	
242	## Minimal Control Surface
243	
244	The shipped supervisor needs only a small operator surface:
245	
246	- project selector or single-project default binding
247	- one-shot mode for a single claim-and-run cycle
248	- continuous loop mode with a poll interval
249	- target branch selection
250	- explicit stop / shutdown handling
251	
252	The contract deliberately does not couple these controls to any future
253	multi-project scheduler. A later multi-project server may host several
254	single-project workers, but each worker still obeys this contract one project
255	at a time.
256	
257	## Observability
258	
259	The supervisor should emit enough state to answer:
260	
261	- which project is being scanned
262	- which bead was selected and why
263	- whether claim, validation, execution, or land failed
264	- which base revision and result revision were used
265	- where the active worktree lives
266	- what hidden ref was created for a preserved attempt
267	- how long the iteration took
268	
269	At minimum, the loop should expose:
270	
271	- current project
272	- current bead ID
273	- current state machine step
274	- last success timestamp
275	- last failure `status`
276	- current worktree path
277	- current execution bundle path
278	- current preserve ref, if any
279	
280	## Acceptance Notes
281	
282	- The contract stays single-project scoped for the first release.
283	- The loop consumes DDx execution validation instead of HELIX-hidden policy.
284	- The supervisor does not create or remove the execution worktree; that
285	  lifecycle remains inside `execute-bead`.
286	- Non-landed attempts are preserved by `execute-bead`, not rewritten by the
287	  supervisor.
288	- Successful attempts land by rebase plus fast-forward inside `execute-bead`
289	  and then reset the worker worktree to the new tip.
290	- The contract remains valid when later `ddx server` work uses project-scoped
291	  worker pools.
292

9	# API/Interface Contract: Epic Worker Surfaces
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	Define the server API, worker model, and UI surfaces for epic-scoped execution.
14	Epic workers differ from single-ticket workers: they own a persistent branch
15	and worktree, execute child beads sequentially, and land via a merge commit
16	rather than individual rebase+ff operations.
17	
18	This contract extends the single-ticket supervisor (API-001) without modifying
19	it. The two worker modes coexist on the same server.
20	
21	## Epic Worker Model
22	
23	An epic worker is a long-lived worker that:
24	
25	1. Claims one open epic bead
26	2. Creates a persistent epic branch: `epic/<bead-id>`
27	3. Creates a persistent worktree at `.ddx/.epic-wt-<bead-id>/`
28	4. Sequentially executes each execution-eligible child bead inside the epic worktree
29	5. After each child completes, commits the result on the epic branch
30	6. When all children are done (or the epic is explicitly finalized), merges the epic branch to the target branch with a regular merge commit
31	7. Cleans up the epic worktree and branch
32	
33	### State Machine
34	
35	```
36	CLAIMED ŌåÆ BRANCHING ŌåÆ EXECUTING_CHILDREN ŌåÆ MERGING ŌåÆ DONE
37	                Ōåæ              Ōöé
38	                ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ RETRY_CHILD (on child failure, continue with next)
39	```
40	
41	- **CLAIMED**: Epic bead claimed, no branch yet
42	- **BRANCHING**: Creating epic branch and worktree
43	- **EXECUTING_CHILDREN**: Running child beads one at a time
44	- **RETRY_CHILD**: A child failed; record result and proceed to next child
45	- **MERGING**: All children done (or stopped), performing epic merge
46	- **DONE**: Epic merged or preserved, worker exits
47	
48	### Child Execution
49	
50	Each child bead executes as a normal `execute-bead` invocation inside the epic
51	worktree, with these differences:
52	
53	- The base revision is the epic branch HEAD (not the project default branch)
54	- The child result is committed directly to the epic branch
55	- There is no individual land/preserve step ŌĆö the child commit stays on the epic branch
56	- Failed children are recorded but do not block the next child
57	- The epic worker may be configured to stop on first child failure or continue
58	
59	### Merge Gate
60	
61	After all children are processed, the epic worker evaluates a merge gate:
62	
63	1. Rebase the epic branch onto the current target branch HEAD
64	2. Run required post-epic checks (if any)
65	3. If clean: merge the epic branch to the target branch with `git merge --no-ff`
66	4. If conflicts: preserve the epic branch under `refs/ddx/epics/<bead-id>/` and report `land_conflict`
67	
68	The merge commit message includes:
69	- Epic bead ID and title
70	- Number of children executed
71	- Number of children succeeded vs failed
72	- Worker ID and timestamp
73	
74	## Server API
75	
76	### Worker Endpoints
77	
78	All worker endpoints are shared between single-ticket and epic workers. The
79	worker record includes a `mode` field to distinguish them.
80	
81	#### `GET /api/agent/workers`
82	
83	List all workers. Supports filtering:
84	
85	| Query param | Values | Description |
86	|---|---|---|
87	| `mode` | `single`, `epic` | Filter by worker mode |
88	| `state` | `running`, `exited` | Filter by state |
89	| `project` | `<project-id>` | Filter by project |
90	
91	Response includes `mode` field on each worker record.
92	
93	#### `GET /api/agent/workers/:id`
94	
95	Show worker details. Epic workers include additional fields:
96	
97	```json
98	{
99	  "mode": "epic",
100	  "epic_bead_id": "ddx-cf340665",
101	  "epic_branch": "epic/ddx-cf340665",
102	  "epic_worktree": ".ddx/.epic-wt-ddx-cf340665",
103	  "children_total": 5,
104	  "children_done": 3,
105	  "children_succeeded": 2,
106	  "children_failed": 1,
107	  "children_remaining": 2,
108	  "current_child": "ddx-abc123"
109	}
110	```
111	
112	Single-ticket workers include `"mode": "single"` and omit the epic fields.
113	
114	#### `POST /api/agent/workers/execute-epic`
115	
116	Start an epic worker. Request body:
117	
118	```json
119	{
120	  "epic_bead_id": "ddx-cf340665",
121	  "project": "ddx",
122	  "model": "z-ai/glm-5.1",
123	  "harness": "agent",
124	  "stop_on_failure": false
125	}
126	```
127	
128	- `stop_on_failure`: if true, stop executing children after the first failure
129	  (default: false ŌĆö continue with remaining children)
130	
131	#### `POST /api/agent/workers/:id/stop`
132	
133	Gracefully stop a running worker. For epic workers, stops after the current
134	child completes. Does not abort mid-execution.
135	
136	### Queue Endpoints
137	
138	#### `GET /api/beads/ready?mode=epic`
139	
140	List execution-ready epic beads. An epic is execution-eligible when:
141	
142	- Status is `open` or `in_progress`
143	- `execution-eligible` is not explicitly `false`
144	- Not in cooldown from a prior attempt
145	- Has at least one execution-eligible child bead
146	
147	#### `GET /api/beads/:id/children`
148	
149	List child beads of an epic. Response includes each child's execution status.
150	
151	### Observability Endpoints
152	
153	#### `GET /api/agent/workers/:id/children`
154	
155	For epic workers, list child execution results:
156	
157	```json
158	[
159	  {
160	    "bead_id": "ddx-abc123",
161	    "status": "success",
162	    "detail": "",
163	    "base_rev": "abc1234",
164	    "result_rev": "def5678",
165	    "attempt_id": "20260412T120000-abc"
166	  },
167	  {
168	    "bead_id": "ddx-def456",
169	    "status": "execution_failed",
170	    "detail": "iteration_limit",
171	    "base_rev": "def5678",
172	    "result_rev": "def5678",
173	    "attempt_id": "20260412T121500-def"
174	  }
175	]
176	```
177	
178	#### `GET /api/agent/workers/:id/log`
179	
180	Same as single-ticket worker log, but also includes child-level progress:
181	
182	```
183	Ō¢Č epic worker started (model: z-ai/glm-5.1)
184	  ­¤ōŗ epic: ddx-cf340665 ŌĆö Design epic-scoped execution and merge workflow
185	  ­¤ī┐ branch: epic/ddx-cf340665
186	  ­¤æČ child 1/5: ddx-abc123
187	  Ō£ō ddx-abc123 ŌåÆ success (abc1234)
188	  ­¤æČ child 2/5: ddx-def456
189	  Ō£Ś ddx-def456 ŌåÆ execution_failed (iteration_limit)
190	  ­¤æČ child 3/5: ddx-ghi789
191	  ...
192	  ­¤öĆ merging epic branch ŌåÆ main
193	  Ō£ō epic merged (5 children: 4 succeeded, 1 failed)
194	```
195	
196	## CLI Surface
197	
198	### `ddx agent execute-epic`
199	
200	```
201	ddx agent execute-epic <epic-bead-id> [flags]
202	
203	Flags:
204	  --model string         Model to use for child execution
205	  --harness string       Agent harness (default: agent)
206	  --stop-on-failure      Stop after first child failure
207	  --local                Run inline instead of submitting to server
208	```
209	
210	### `ddx server workers list --mode epic`
211	
212	Filter worker list to epic workers only.
213	
214	### `ddx server workers show <id>`
215	
216	Works for both modes. Epic workers show child progress.
217	
218	## Worker Record Schema
219	
220	The existing `WorkerRecord` gains these fields:
221	
222	```go
223	type WorkerRecord struct {
224	    // ... existing fields ...
225	    Mode            WorkerMode  `json:"mode"`              // "single" or "epic"
226	    EpicBeadID      string      `json:"epic_bead_id,omitempty"`
227	    EpicBranch      string      `json:"epic_branch,omitempty"`
228	    EpicWorktree    string      `json:"epic_worktree,omitempty"`
229	    ChildrenTotal   int         `json:"children_total,omitempty"`
230	    ChildrenDone    int         `json:"children_done,omitempty"`
231	    ChildrenSuccess int         `json:"children_succeeded,omitempty"`
232	    ChildrenFailed  int         `json:"children_failed,omitempty"`
233	    CurrentChild    string      `json:"current_child,omitempty"`
234	}
235	
236	type WorkerMode string
237	
238	const (
239	    WorkerModeSingle WorkerMode = "single"
240	    WorkerModeEpic   WorkerMode = "epic"
241	)
242	```
243	
244	## Distinction From Single-Ticket Workers
245	
246	| Aspect | Single-Ticket Worker | Epic Worker |
247	|---|---|---|
248	| Queue source | `ReadyExecution()` | `ReadyExecution()` filtered by `issue_type=epic` |
249	| Beads per run | 1 | All children of 1 epic |
250	| Branch strategy | Per-attempt hidden ref | Persistent `epic/<id>` branch |
251	| Worktree lifetime | Created/destroyed per attempt | Persistent for epic duration |
252	| Landing | Rebase + ff per attempt | Single `--no-ff` merge after all children |
253	| Failure mode | Single result | Partial: some children may succeed |
254	| Cooldown | Per-bead retry-after | Per-epic retry-after |
255	
256	## Acceptance
257	
258	1. Server API exposes `mode`, epic fields, and child results on worker records
259	2. `ddx agent execute-epic` creates a persistent epic branch and worktree
260	3. Child beads execute sequentially inside the epic worktree
261	4. Epic worker log shows per-child progress with aggregate status
262	5. Merge gate rebases epic branch and creates merge commit on success
263	6. Failed epic merges preserve the branch under `refs/ddx/epics/`
264	7. Single-ticket workers are unaffected ŌĆö the two modes share infrastructure but not state
265

10	# Solution Design: Multi-Agent Coordination
11	
12	## Purpose
13	
14	This design specifies how DDx makes multi-agent and multi-machine work safe
15	and observable without becoming an orchestration framework. Coordination
16	policy stays in HELIX and other workflow tools; DDx provides the primitives.
17	
18	## Scope
19	
20	- Machine/session identity in bead claims
21	- MCP write tools for beads (remote supervisor surface)
22	- Worktree-aware agent dispatch
23	- Git as the coordination bus
24	- Division of responsibility between DDx and workflow tools
25	
26	Out of scope: machine provisioning, process supervision, token budget
27	management, automatic merge conflict resolution.
28	
29	## Bead Concurrency: Machine/Session Identity in Claims
30	
31	Today claims record `claimed-pid` and `assignee`. This is insufficient for
32	multi-machine use because PIDs collide across hosts and `assignee` is advisory.
33	
34	**Changes to the claim record:**
35	
36	- Add `claimed-machine` (hostname or `$DDX_MACHINE_ID` env override)
37	- Add `claimed-session` (agent session ID if invoked from `ddx agent run`,
38	  empty otherwise)
39	- Add `claimed-worktree` (worktree branch name when `--worktree` is set)
40	
41	These three fields augment rather than replace the existing claim fields. The
42	claim algorithm in SD-004 is unchanged; the new fields are populated at step 4
43	alongside `claimed-at` and `claimed-pid`.
44	
45	Claims remain advisory. No hard reservation lock is introduced. The three new
46	fields allow a coordinator to answer: "which machine, session, and branch holds
47	this bead?" without polling external state.
48	
49	**Concurrency safety:** The existing atomic temp-file swap and directory lock
50	(SD-004) already serialize concurrent writers. No changes to the write path are
51	required. Multiple agents racing to claim the same bead will serialize; the
52	last writer wins, which is the current behavior.
53	
54	## MCP Supervisor Surface
55	
56	`ddx server` (FEAT-002) today exposes read-only bead and document endpoints.
57	A remote supervisor needs write access to steer work without running the CLI
58	locally.
59	
60	**MCP write tools (specified in FEAT-002, implemented by this SD):**
61	
62	FEAT-002 already specifies the following bead mutation tools (FEAT-002 lines
63	87ŌĆō92). This SD defines their server-side implementation details.
64	
65	| Tool | Description |
66	|------|-------------|
67	| `ddx_bead_create` | Create a bead in the active-work collection |
68	| `ddx_bead_update` | Update status, title, or labels on an existing bead |
69	| `ddx_bead_claim` | Claim or unclaim a bead with caller identity |
70	
71	These tools are thin wrappers over the existing bead store methods. They use
72	the same atomic write path as the CLI. No new storage logic is introduced.
73	
74	**Existing read tools remain unchanged.** The supervisor can already read bead
75	state, execution results, and agent session logs via existing MCP resources.
76	
77	**Authentication:** Non-localhost access is authenticated via ts-net (Tailscale),
78	per ADR-006. The server binds to localhost by default; enabling `server.tsnet`
79	in `.ddx/config.yaml` adds a Tailscale listener with identity-based auth at
80	the transport layer. No API keys or custom auth middleware are required.
81	
82	**MCP notifications (future):** Real-time notifications on state change (bead
83	transitions, execution completions) are deferred. The supervisor can poll bead
84	state via existing list/show endpoints in the near term.
85	
86	## Worktree-Aware Dispatch
87	
88	`ddx agent run` today accepts `--workdir` to set the working directory of the
89	agent invocation.
90	
91	**New flag:** `--worktree <name>`
92	
93	Behavior:
94	1. If the named worktree does not exist, create it with
95	   `git worktree add .worktrees/<name> -b <name>`.
96	2. Set the agent working directory to the worktree path.
97	3. Populate `claimed-worktree` on any bead claim made during the run.
98	4. `ddx bead show` renders `claimed-worktree` alongside other claim fields.
99	
100	The `--workdir` flag continues to work unchanged. `--worktree` is additive.
101	
102	## Git as Coordination Bus
103	
104	DDx does not introduce a coordination service. All durable state lives in
105	git-tracked files. The coordination pattern for multi-machine work:
106	
107	1. Coordinator creates a worktree branch and pushes it to the remote.
108	2. Worker (another machine or account) fetches the branch and runs
109	   `ddx agent run --worktree <branch>`.
110	3. Worker pushes results (code, updated beads, agent session logs) to the branch.
111	4. Coordinator fetches, reviews, and merges.
112	
113	**What DDx provides in this flow:**
114	- `ddx bead claim` records which branch/machine holds the work
115	- `ddx agent run --worktree` creates the isolation boundary
116	- Bead JSONL and session collections are file-backed and merge-friendly
117	  (append-only JSONL rows are non-conflicting if agents write to separate
118	  collections or the merge strategy takes both sides)
119	
120	**What DDx does not provide:**
121	- Push/pull scheduling or automation
122	- Branch merge strategy
123	- Conflict resolution policy
124	
125	## What DDx Provides vs. Workflow Tools
126	
127	| Concern | Owner |
128	|---------|-------|
129	| Safe concurrent bead writes | DDx (atomic swap + lock) |
130	| Machine/session identity in claims | DDx (claim fields) |
131	| Worktree creation and dispatch | DDx (`--worktree` flag) |
132	| MCP read surface for remote supervisors | DDx (existing server) |
133	| MCP write tools for beads | DDx (new tools, this design) |
134	| Which agent works on which bead | HELIX / workflow policy |
135	| When to merge a worktree branch | HELIX / workflow policy |
136	| Remote machine provisioning | Out of scope |
137	| Token budget management | Out of scope |
138	
139	## Reference Systems
140	
141	**Gastown** (anthropics/gastown): Demonstrates multi-agent work distribution
142	using claim semantics and a shared queue. The `claimed-machine` / `claimed-session`
143	pattern here mirrors Gastown's worker identity approach.
144	
145	**MCP Agent Mailbox** (anthropics/agent-mailbox): Demonstrates async agent-to-
146	agent messaging over MCP. The MCP write tools for beads (`ddx_bead_claim`,
147	`ddx_bead_update`) provide a similar coordination surface without introducing
148	a separate mailbox service ŌĆö beads are the messages.
149	
150	## Validation
151	
152	- `TestBeadClaimRecordsMachineAndSession` ŌĆö claim fields populated correctly
153	- `TestBeadClaimWorktreeBranch` ŌĆö worktree name stored in claim record
154	- `TestMCPBeadCreateTool` ŌĆö MCP create tool writes via store
155	- `TestMCPBeadClaimTool` ŌĆö MCP claim tool respects lock and atomic write
156	- `TestAgentRunWorktreeFlag` ŌĆö creates worktree, sets workdir, records branch
157	- `TestAgentRunWorktreeFlagReusesExisting` ŌĆö second run reuses existing worktree
158

12	# Feature: Multi-Agent and Multi-Machine Coordination
13	
14	**ID:** FEAT-013
15	**Status:** Framing
16	**Priority:** P1
17	**Owner:** DDx Team
18	
19	## Overview
20	
21	DDx needs to be safe and useful in a world where multiple agents ŌĆö possibly
22	on different machines, using different models, working in parallel worktrees ŌĆö
23	interact with the same project. On each machine, `ddx server` runs as a
24	per-user host daemon: one server process per machine, holding a host+user
25	project registry in `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json` (FEAT-020) and
26	serving every registered project from a single process. Inside that one
27	daemon, the in-process `WorkerManager` supervises execute-loop workers in
28	goroutines, one worker per project context. This is not cluster
29	orchestration. DDx stays local-first and git-native. But it must design for
30	coordination at the edges: safe concurrent writes, observable state, and
31	composable dispatch patterns.
32	
33	## Problem Statement
34	
35	**Current situation:**
36	- A human supervisor (the "side channel") runs alongside implementation
37	  agents, reviewing specs, evaluating test results, and steering beads. This
38	  works today by running `helix worker` as a background process locally.
39	- Sub-agents can be spawned locally in worktrees for parallel implementation,
40	  but merging their results is manual.
41	- There is no way to spread work across machines or agent accounts (for token
42	  budget distribution, compile/test parallelism, or model diversity).
43	- Concurrent bead updates from multiple agents can race on the JSONL store.
44	- Execute-loop workers and their lifecycle (start/logs/stop) were not
45	  previously visible to a remote supervisor; they now run inside the host+user
46	  `ddx-server` as supervised goroutines, but the coordination primitives for
47	  multi-agent work still need to be spelled out.
48	
49	**Desired outcome:** DDx provides the primitives that make multi-agent
50	work safe and observable, without becoming an orchestration framework.
51	Orchestration policy stays in HELIX and other workflow tools.
52	
53	## User Scenarios
54	
55	### S1: Supervisory Side Channel (Human-in-the-Loop)
56	
57	**Today:** A developer runs `helix worker` in the background while working in
58	a separate terminal (or Claude Code session). They review execution results,
59	update specs, evaluate bead completion, and steer the queue. Both the worker
60	and the supervisor operate on the same repo.
61	
62	**Near-term need:** The supervisor should be able to operate from a different
63	machine or session without conflicting with the worker. This means:
64	- Bead state must be safe for concurrent read/write (already mostly true
65	  with append-only JSONL + atomic writes)
66	- Spec document edits from the supervisor must auto-commit (FEAT-012) so the
67	  worker sees them
68	- The supervisor needs read access to agent session logs and execution results
69	  without being co-located
70	
71	**Is this an MCP server use case?** Yes. `ddx server` (FEAT-002) already
72	exposes beads, documents, agent sessions, and execution results over MCP and
73	HTTP. A supervisor on another machine can connect to the DDx MCP server to:
74	- Read bead state and queue status
75	- Read execution results and test output
76	- Read/write spec documents (FEAT-012)
77	- Inspect agent session logs
78	
79	The missing pieces are:
80	- MCP write tools for beads (today beads are CLI-only writes)
81	- MCP notifications when state changes (bead created, execution completed)
82	- Authentication for non-localhost access
83	
84	### S2: Federated Building in Parallel Worktrees
85	
86	**Today:** A planner agent spawns sub-agents in git worktrees for orthogonal
87	implementation tasks. Each worktree is a full copy of the repo. When the
88	sub-agent finishes, the worktree is merged back (or abandoned if the work
89	failed).
90	
91	**Near-term need:** This pattern should work across machines:
92	1. A coordinator creates a worktree branch for a task
93	2. Pushes the branch to the remote
94	3. A worker (on another machine, possibly another agent account) clones/fetches
95	   that branch, does the work, pushes results
96	4. The coordinator merges the results
97	
98	**What DDx provides:**
99	- `ddx bead claim` already supports agent coordination (mark who's working
100	  on what)
101	- Bead state tracks which worktree/branch a bead is being worked in
102	- `ddx checkpoint` (FEAT-012) marks known-good states before/after merges
103	- `ddx agent run` can target a specific worktree via `--workdir`
104	
105	**What DDx does NOT provide:**
106	- Machine provisioning or remote execution
107	- Branch merge strategy (that's git + workflow policy)
108	- Token budget management across accounts
109	- Process supervision (that's the OS/scheduler)
110	
111	### S3: Observability Across Agents
112	
113	Multiple agents working in parallel need a shared view of:
114	- Which beads are claimed, by whom, in which worktree
115	- Execution results from all agents' runs
116	- Spec document changes made by any agent
117	
118	This is already mostly covered by the bead tracker and execution store being
119	file-backed and git-synced. The gaps are:
120	- Real-time notifications (agent A finishes a bead ŌåÆ agent B sees it
121	  immediately)
122	- Cross-worktree bead visibility (worktree A's bead updates aren't visible
123	  in worktree B until merged)
124	
125	## Design Principles
126	
127	### 1. Git is the coordination bus
128	
129	All durable state lives in git-tracked files. Agents on different machines
130	coordinate by pushing/pulling branches. DDx does not introduce a separate
131	coordination service, message queue, or database.
132	
133	### 2. Beads are the work distribution primitive
134	
135	Beads with claim semantics are how work is allocated to agents. The
136	`claim/unclaim` pattern with PID tracking already prevents double-execution.
137	Extending this to include machine identity and branch tracking makes it
138	multi-machine safe.
139	
140	### 3. MCP server is the remote observation surface
141	
142	`ddx server` provides read (and limited write) access to project state for
143	remote supervisors and tools. This is the "side channel" that supervisors
144	use. It does not replace the CLI for heavy operations.
145	
146	The server is a per-user host daemon: one instance per machine, with a
147	host+user project registry persisted at
148	`~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json` (FEAT-020). Every request and tool
149	call is resolved against one selected project context before the adapter
150	runs. Execute-loop workers are supervised inside the same process by the
151	server's `WorkerManager`, so a remote supervisor that talks to one host+user
152	daemon can observe and coordinate every worker, bead, session, and execution
153	attempt on that machine through a single project-scoped API surface.
154	
155	### 4. Worktrees are the isolation boundary
156	
157	Each agent works in its own worktree. Merging is explicit. This avoids
158	concurrent file writes entirely ŌĆö the only shared mutable state is git refs.
159	In a multi-project server, the worktree boundary is also project-scoped so one
160	project's worktree operations never reuse another project's checkout root.
161	
162	### 5. DDx provides primitives, not orchestration
163	
164	DDx does not decide which agent works on which bead, when to merge, or how
165	to handle conflicts. That's workflow-level policy (HELIX). DDx provides:
166	- Safe concurrent bead operations
167	- Observable state over MCP/HTTP
168	- Worktree-aware agent dispatch
169	- Git-backed coordination primitives
170	
171	## Requirements
172	
173	### Functional (Near-term)
174	
175	**Bead concurrency safety**
176	1. Bead claims include machine/session identity, not just PID
177	2. MCP write tools for beads: `ddx_bead_create`, `ddx_bead_update`,
178	   `ddx_bead_claim`
179	3. Bead operations safe under concurrent append to JSONL
180	   (already implemented via atomic writes ŌĆö verify and document)
181	
182	**MCP supervisor surface**
183	4. MCP tools for execution result inspection
184	5. MCP write tools for documents (FEAT-012)
185	6. MCP write tools for beads (new)
186	7. Optional authentication for non-localhost MCP access
187	
188	**Worktree-aware dispatch**
189	8. `ddx agent run --worktree <name>` creates/reuses a worktree for the run
190	9. Bead claim records the worktree branch
191	10. `ddx bead show` displays the active worktree/branch for claimed beads
192	
193	### Functional (Future ŌĆö framing only)
194	
195	**Remote worker pattern**
196	11. A coordinator can publish a "work package" (bead + branch + prompt) that
197	    a remote worker picks up
198	12. Workers report results by pushing to the branch and updating the bead
199	13. The coordinator merges completed branches
200	
201	**Notification**
202	14. MCP server emits notifications on state changes (bead transitions,
203	    execution completions)
204	15. Subscribers can filter by label, status, or artifact ID
205	
206	### Non-Functional
207	
208	- **No new infrastructure:** Coordination uses git + existing MCP server.
209	  No message queues, databases, or orchestration services.
210	- **Degrade gracefully:** Single-machine, single-agent use is the default.
211	  Multi-agent features add capability without adding complexity for solo users.
212	- **Token budget awareness:** DDx should track token usage per agent session
213	  (already in agent logs) but does not manage budgets or accounts.
214	
215	## Reference Systems
216	
217	| System | Relevance |
218	|--------|-----------|
219	| [Gastown](https://github.com/anthropics/gastown) | Multi-agent coordination patterns, work distribution |
220	| [MCP Agent Mailbox](https://github.com/anthropics/agent-mailbox) | Async agent-to-agent messaging over MCP |
221	| Git worktrees | Isolation boundary for parallel work |
222	| Git push/pull | Coordination bus for multi-machine work |
223

From **FEAT-010 (Executions)** - Lines 79-99:
- Execution definitions "may be authored as git-tracked documents (graph-authored definitions, discovered via FEAT-007) or managed as DDx runtime records (runtime-managed definitions stored in the `exec-definitions` collection)."
- Graph-authored definitions are "the preferred source when using `ddx agent execute-bead`"

From **SD-013 (Multi-Agent Coordination)** - Lines 61-72:

The specifications indicate that workers should create sub-tasks **out-of-band** via the **MCP `ddx_bead_create` tool**, not inline during execution:

From **bead.go** (Lines 243-308) - The bead creation command shows:
```go
func (f *CommandFactory) newBeadCreateCommand() *cobra.Command {
    cmd := &cobra.Command{
        Use:   "create <title>",
        Short: "Create a new bead",
        ...
    }
    // ... creation logic with Title, IssueType, Priority, Labels, etc.
    if err := s.Create(b); err != nil {
        return err
    }
    f.beadAutoCommit("create " + b.ID)
    fmt.Fprintf(cmd.OutOrStdout(), "%s\n", b.ID)
}
```

**Workers should use the MCP surface** (from SD-013):
- Remote supervisors can call `ddx_bead_create` to spawn sub-tasks
- Agents running locally can shell-execute `ddx bead create` via the CLI
- The bead store methods are atomic and safe for concurrent writes

### 6. Documentation About Worker Sub-Task Creation

**From API-001 (Execute-Bead Supervisor Contract):**
- Lines 22-57: Workers execute "one ordinary executable bead at a time"
- Lines 116-125: Supervisor observes bead outcomes from documented result schema
- No mention of in-bead task creation ŌĆö workers focus on execution, not orchestration

**From API-002 (Epic Worker Surfaces):**
- Lines 48-58: Epic workers execute child beads **sequentially**
- Child beads pre-exist; epic workers don't create them
- Epic branch tracks results; children are committed to the epic branch

**Key principle from FEAT-013:**
- "DDx provides primitives, not orchestration" (line 162)
- "Orchestration policy stays in HELIX and other workflow tools" (lines 50-51)
- Workers should focus on task execution; supervisors decide decomposition

**Conclusion:** DDx does not currently support in-band bead creation by workers during execution. The intended pattern is:
1. Supervisors (human or external orchestrator) create sub-beads via CLI or MCP
2. Workers claim and execute from the ready queue
3. Results are committed and merged through the landing orchestrator
4. No "spawn sub-task on-the-fly" capability exists in the execute-bead worker code itself

7	# Feature: DDx Agent Service (consumer of ddx-agent contract)
8	
9	**ID:** FEAT-006
10	**Status:** In Progress (migrating to ddx-agent CONTRACT-003)
11	**Priority:** P0
12	**Owner:** DDx Team
13	
14	## Overview
15	
16	DDx invokes LLMs through the `ddx-agent` module, defined by
17	**CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service** in the `~/Projects/agent` repo
18	(`docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md`).
19	
20	That contract is the entire boundary. DDx's CLI commands (`ddx agent run`,
21	`ddx agent execute-bead`, `ddx work`, `ddx agent providers`, `ddx agent models`,
22	`ddx agent route-status`, `ddx agent check`) are thin wrappers that call into
23	the `agentlib.DdxAgent` interface and render its results.
24	
25	## DDx-side responsibilities
26	
27	DDx owns the bead-driven workflow surface. It does NOT own:
28	- Harness orchestration (claude, codex, opencode, pi, gemini, native)
29	- Provider routing
30	- Model catalog
31	- Compaction
32	- Tool registration
33	- Session-log shape
34	
35	Those all live inside ddx-agent per CONTRACT-003.
36	
37	DDx owns:
38	
39	- **Bead-driven invocation.** `ddx agent execute-bead`, `ddx work`, and the
40	  server's queue-drain worker translate bead state into `ExecuteRequest`
41	  values and surface results back into the bead tracker.
42	- **Execute-bead orchestration.** Worktree creation, base-revision pinning,
43	  result landing (merge / preserve / no-changes), gate evaluation, evidence
44	  bundle capture. The agent provides the LLM execution; DDx provides the
45	  git-aware orchestration.
46	- **Evidence and session capture.** DDx writes `.ddx/executions/<id>/`
47	  bundles with prompts, manifests, and result artifacts. The agent's session
48	  log path (returned in `ExecuteResponse.SessionLogPath`) is captured into
49	  the bundle.
50	- **Profile policy at the request level.** DDx selects `Profile`, `Effort`,
51	  `Permissions` per bead (or per CLI invocation) based on bead metadata
52	  and user input. The agent receives those as `ExecuteRequest` fields and
53	  performs the routing.
54	
55	## Profile Routing
56	
57	`ddx work --profile default` is the primary queue-drain invocation. DDx maps
58	the user-facing profile to an ordered tier ladder and resolves each tier through
59	the agent service with the configured provider affinity and capability gating.
60	
61	| Profile | Ladder | Intent |
62	| --- | --- | --- |
63	| `default` | `[cheap, standard, smart]` | Local first, then subscription cloud, then high-quality cloud when earlier tiers fail. This is the common throughput-per-dollar path. |
64	| `cheap` | `[cheap]` | Local only. Never escalates; if local cannot serve the bead, the bead is unclaimed and waits. |
65	| `fast` | `[fast, smart]` | Cloud-fast first, skipping local warmup; escalates to smart on failure. |
66	| `smart` | `[smart]` | High-quality cloud from the start. No escalation. |
67	
68	The `.ddx/config.yaml` field `agent.routing.profile_ladders` can override the
69	ordered tier list per profile. The legacy flat `agent.routing.profile_priority`
70	is still read as the `default` profile fallback and emits a deprecation warning;
71	new configs should use `profile_ladders.default`. `agent.routing.model_overrides`
72	can map a ladder tier such as `cheap`, `standard`, `fast`, or `smart` to a
73	concrete model reference before DDx asks the agent service to resolve the route.
74	
75	Escalation advances to the next tier for `execution_failed`,
76	`land_conflict`, `post_run_check_failed`, and
77	`structural_validation_failed`. `no_changes` keeps the existing cooldown and
78	satisfaction-adjudication path rather than consuming a higher tier.
79	
80	### Conflict-recovery structured outcomes (bead ddx-0097af14)
81	
82	Before parking a bead on `land_conflict` (or on `execution_failed` when the
83	agent produced a commit before failing), the execute-loop now attempts to reuse
84	the preserved iteration ref rather than discarding the agent's work:
85	
86	1. **3-way ort auto-merge** (`git merge --no-ff -s ort -X ours`). If clean,
87	   the bead closes as `success`.
88	2. **Focused conflict-resolve agent** (if `ConflictResolver` is configured on
89	   the worker). If clean, the bead closes as `success`.
90	3. **Park with structured outcome.** Two new statuses replace the generic
91	   reopen when both recovery paths fail:
92	
93	| Status | Event kind | Meaning |
94	| --- | --- | --- |
95	| `land_conflict_unresolvable` | `land-conflict-unresolvable` | All auto-recovery failed; retryable after 15-min cooldown. |
96	| `land_conflict_needs_human` | `land-conflict-needs-human` | Focused-resolve agent returned BLOCKING; human input required. |
97	
98	Both statuses use `LandConflictCooldown` (15 min) rather than the 24h cap used
99	for `push_failed`, because land conflicts typically unblock quickly as sibling
100	beads advance the base branch.
101	
102	## Asking ddx-agent for changes
103	
104	When DDx needs new behavior from the agent ŌĆö a new method, a new field on
105	`ExecuteRequest`, a new event type, a new policy knob ŌĆö file a PR against
106	CONTRACT-003 in the agent repo. Maintainers decide whether the surface grows.
107	
108	Do not import agent internal packages. They live under `internal/` and the
109	Go compiler blocks external imports after agent v0.5.0 ships.
110	
111	## Migration status
112	
113	DDx is currently migrating from the legacy DDx-internal routing/harness
114	implementation to the CONTRACT-003 surface. Migration epic: `ddx-ac5c7fdb`.
115	Pre-migration, FEAT-006 documented harness orchestration responsibilities
116	that have since moved upstream.
117	
118	## Design References
119	
120	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-006-agent-session-capture.md` ŌĆö session log shape and evidence bundles
121	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-013-multi-agent-coordination.md` ŌĆö worktree-aware dispatch, concurrent bead safety
122	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-014-token-awareness.md` ŌĆö provider availability, quota, and signal model
123	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md` ŌĆö bead-level cost and rework derivation
124	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-020-multi-machine-coordinator-topology.md` ŌĆö host+user coordinator scoping
125	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-021-service-backed-multi-node-topology.md` ŌĆö service-backed node topology
126	- `docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-006-agent-session-capture.md` ŌĆö session log and artifact format
127	- `docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-010-executions.md` ŌĆö execution attempt bundles at `.ddx/executions/`
128	- `docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-18-ddx-agent-service-interface.md` ŌĆö current thin-consumer migration plan
129	- `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-006-agent-session-capture.md` ŌĆö session capture test coverage
130	- `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-014-token-awareness.md` ŌĆö token-awareness coverage
131	- `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-020-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md` ŌĆö routing and catalog resolution coverage
132
